paragraphs.text: Greetings to the Comission and all parties reading this message, I think it would be of great importance if the Digital Fairness Act moved to include video game preservation in its legislation. Preserving the right of people to own the digital media that they own is of central importance to the DFA and as such, I think that extending these protections to video games would go a long way to ensuring consumers who've paid money and legally own digital media such as video games, would enjoy the same protections against companies that seek to undermine the basic consumer right of ownership. I only ask you to consider the following example: That CD I bought of a Beatles album 20 years ago, still works perfectly fine if the disk has not been damaged. Since I own the disk, I have been given the right to use it (for my personal use only of course). A video game that I bought 5 years ago, suddenly has it's Digital Rights Management server go offline and as such, has become unplayable, even though I legally purchased a copy and own it. Why must that be the case? Why should companies charge full price for video games and reserve the right to terminate my ability to play that game, even though I have legally obtained a copy of it? We ask you to seriously consider protecting video game preservation and making sure companies are held accountable for after end-of-life support, through the Digital Fairness Act! Video games are art! How would you feel if you bought a vinyl record that suddenly stopped working after 10 years because a company decided to revoke your right of ownership!
